verdict: Tirzepatide shows greater weight loss in head-to-head trials (~22% vs ~15%), but semaglutide has a longer track record and more availability. Cost is similar for compounded versions.

bestFor.medA: Patients who want a well-studied medication with oral option
bestFor.medB: Patients seeking maximum weight loss with dual-action mechanism

headToHeadTrials: SURMOUNT-5 showed tirzepatide achieved greater weight loss than semaglutide

faqQuestions.q: Is tirzepatide better than semaglutide?
faqQuestions.a: Clinical trials suggest tirzepatide produces more weight loss on average (~22% vs ~15%), but individual results vary. Both are effective GLP-1 medications.

faqQuestions.q: Can you switch from semaglutide to tirzepatide?
faqQuestions.a: Yes, with your doctor's guidance. Typically you'd start tirzepatide at the lowest dose even if you were on a high dose of semaglutide.

faqQuestions.q: Which has fewer side effects?
faqQuestions.a: Both have similar GI side effect profiles. Some studies suggest tirzepatide may cause slightly less nausea at equivalent efficacy doses.

medA.name: Ozempic
uniquePros: Covered by more insurance for diabetes

verdict: Ozempic and Wegovy contain the same active ingredient (semaglutide). Wegovy is FDA-approved for weight loss at a higher dose (2.4mg vs 2mg) and has more weight-loss data. Ozempic is approved for type 2 diabetes and is often prescribed off-label for weight loss.

bestFor.medA: Patients with type 2 diabetes seeking weight loss as a secondary benefit
bestFor.medB: Patients primarily seeking FDA-approved weight management treatment

headToHeadTrials: No direct head-to-head; same drug at different doses. STEP trials used Wegovy dosing (2.4mg).

faqQuestions.q: Are Ozempic and Wegovy the same drug?
faqQuestions.a: Yes, both contain semaglutide made by Novo Nordisk. Ozempic is approved for type 2 diabetes (max 2mg), while Wegovy is approved for weight management (max 2.4mg).

faqQuestions.q: Can I use Ozempic instead of Wegovy for weight loss?
faqQuestions.a: Many doctors prescribe Ozempic off-label for weight loss. However, Wegovy reaches a higher dose (2.4mg vs 2mg) and is specifically approved for obesity treatment.

faqQuestions.q: Why is Ozempic cheaper than Wegovy?
faqQuestions.a: Insurance coverage differs because Ozempic is approved for diabetes (commonly covered) while Wegovy is for weight management (less commonly covered). The list prices are similar.

medB.name: Mounjaro
uniquePros: Dual mechanism targets GLP-1 and GIP
uniqueCons: Newer drug with less long-term data

verdict: Mounjaro (tirzepatide) achieves significantly more weight loss than Ozempic (semaglutide) due to its dual GLP-1/GIP mechanism. Both are FDA-approved for type 2 diabetes. For weight loss, Mounjaro is used off-label while its sister product Zepbound has the obesity approval.

bestFor.medA: Patients wanting a proven diabetes medication with good weight-loss side benefit
bestFor.medB: Patients with diabetes who also want maximum weight reduction

headToHeadTrials: SURPASS-2 compared Mounjaro to Ozempic 1mg — tirzepatide showed superior A1C reduction and weight loss at all doses.

faqQuestions.q: Is Mounjaro better than Ozempic?
faqQuestions.a: For weight loss, clinical data shows Mounjaro (tirzepatide) produces greater weight reduction than Ozempic (semaglutide). For blood sugar control, Mounjaro also showed superior A1C reduction in the SURPASS-2 trial.

faqQuestions.q: Can I switch from Ozempic to Mounjaro?
faqQuestions.a: Yes, your doctor can transition you. You would typically start Mounjaro at the lowest dose (2.5mg) regardless of your Ozempic dose, then titrate up.

faqQuestions.q: Do they have the same side effects?
faqQuestions.a: Both share similar GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, vomiting). The frequency is comparable, though individual experiences vary.

medB.name: Zepbound
uniqueCons: No cardiovascular outcomes data yet for obesity indication
Newer with less long-term safety data

verdict: Zepbound (tirzepatide) produces more weight loss than Wegovy (semaglutide) — roughly 22% vs 15%. Wegovy has cardiovascular outcomes data from the SELECT trial. Zepbound has a lower list price. Both are FDA-approved specifically for weight management.

bestFor.medA: Patients who prioritize cardiovascular protection and a longer safety track record
bestFor.medB: Patients seeking maximum weight loss with the FDA-approved obesity indication

headToHeadTrials: SURMOUNT-5 directly compared tirzepatide to semaglutide for obesity, showing tirzepatide produced greater weight loss.

faqQuestions.q: Is Zepbound better than Wegovy for weight loss?
faqQuestions.a: Clinical trials show Zepbound (tirzepatide) achieves ~22% weight loss vs ~15% for Wegovy (semaglutide). However, individual results vary and both are highly effective.

faqQuestions.q: Which is cheaper, Wegovy or Zepbound?
faqQuestions.a: Zepbound has a lower list price (~$1,060/mo vs ~$1,350/mo for Wegovy). Actual out-of-pocket costs depend on your insurance coverage and available savings programs.

faqQuestions.q: Does insurance cover Wegovy or Zepbound?
faqQuestions.a: Coverage varies widely. Some insurers cover one but not the other. Both manufacturers offer savings cards that can reduce costs significantly for eligible patients.

medA.name: Semaglutide
uniqueCons: More potent GI side effects initially

medB.name: Liraglutide
uniquePros: Longest GLP-1 track record for obesity

verdict: Semaglutide produces nearly twice the weight loss of liraglutide (~15% vs ~8%) with the convenience of weekly vs daily injections. Liraglutide has a longer safety record and daily dosing can allow more gradual titration for sensitive patients.

bestFor.medA: Most patients — better efficacy and convenience with weekly dosing
bestFor.medB: Patients who prefer daily dosing for gradual adjustment or cannot tolerate semaglutide

headToHeadTrials: STEP 8 compared semaglutide 2.4mg weekly to liraglutide 3.0mg daily — semaglutide achieved 15.8% vs 6.4% weight loss.

faqQuestions.q: Is semaglutide better than liraglutide?
faqQuestions.a: For weight loss, semaglutide is significantly more effective (~15% vs ~8%) and requires only weekly injections instead of daily. The STEP 8 trial directly confirmed semaglutide's superiority.

faqQuestions.q: Why would anyone choose liraglutide over semaglutide?
faqQuestions.a: Some patients prefer daily dosing for finer dose control, or they may not tolerate semaglutide. Liraglutide also has the longest safety track record among GLP-1s for obesity.

faqQuestions.q: Can you switch from liraglutide to semaglutide?
faqQuestions.a: Yes. Your doctor will typically stop liraglutide and start semaglutide at the lowest dose (0.25mg weekly), then titrate up over several weeks.

medA.name: Tirzepatide
uniquePros: Greatest weight loss of any GLP-1

verdict: Tirzepatide achieves nearly three times the weight loss of liraglutide (~22% vs ~8%) with the added convenience of weekly dosing. Liraglutide remains an option for patients who need the longest safety track record or adolescent approval.

bestFor.medA: Patients seeking maximum weight loss with a next-generation medication
bestFor.medB: Adolescents or patients requiring the most-studied first-generation GLP-1

headToHeadTrials: No direct head-to-head trial. Cross-trial comparisons show tirzepatide achieves approximately 2.5-3x the weight loss.

faqQuestions.q: How much more weight loss does tirzepatide cause vs liraglutide?
faqQuestions.a: Based on clinical trial data, tirzepatide produces roughly 22% body weight loss compared to about 8% for liraglutide — nearly three times as much.

faqQuestions.q: Is liraglutide still worth taking?
faqQuestions.a: Liraglutide can still be a good option for patients who want the longest-studied GLP-1, prefer daily dosing flexibility, or cannot access newer medications.

faqQuestions.q: Can you switch from Saxenda to Mounjaro?
faqQuestions.a: Yes, with your doctor's guidance. You would stop Saxenda and start Mounjaro at the lowest dose (2.5mg weekly), then titrate up over several months.

verdict: Ozempic (semaglutide) produces more weight loss than Saxenda (liraglutide) with the convenience of weekly dosing, even though Ozempic is technically off-label for obesity. Saxenda has the advantage of being FDA-approved for weight management and approved for teens.

bestFor.medA: Adults wanting more effective weight loss with weekly convenience
bestFor.medB: Adolescents or patients who need an FDA-approved weight loss medication specifically

headToHeadTrials: STEP 8 compared semaglutide 2.4mg to liraglutide 3.0mg and showed semaglutide was far more effective.

faqQuestions.q: Is Ozempic better than Saxenda?
faqQuestions.a: For weight loss, studies show semaglutide (Ozempic's ingredient) achieves roughly twice the weight loss of liraglutide (Saxenda's ingredient). Ozempic also has the advantage of weekly dosing.

faqQuestions.q: Why is Saxenda more expensive than Ozempic?
faqQuestions.a: Saxenda is priced as an obesity medication while Ozempic is a diabetes drug. Insurance coverage varies — many plans cover Ozempic for diabetes but not Saxenda for weight loss.

faqQuestions.q: Can I take Saxenda and Ozempic together?
faqQuestions.a: No, you should not take two GLP-1 receptor agonists simultaneously. Both work on the same receptor, and combining them increases side effect risk without added benefit.

verdict: Wegovy is the clear upgrade over Saxenda — nearly twice the weight loss (~15% vs ~8%) with weekly instead of daily injections. Both are FDA-approved for weight management. Saxenda remains relevant for adolescents and when Wegovy is unavailable.

bestFor.medA: Most adults seeking FDA-approved weight management with maximum efficacy
bestFor.medB: Adolescents (12+) or patients who cannot access Wegovy due to supply issues

headToHeadTrials: STEP 8 directly compared semaglutide 2.4mg to liraglutide 3.0mg — semaglutide showed 15.8% vs 6.4% weight loss.

faqQuestions.q: Should I switch from Saxenda to Wegovy?
faqQuestions.a: If you want greater weight loss with less frequent injections, Wegovy is the upgrade. Discuss with your doctor — they'll typically start you at the lowest Wegovy dose and titrate up.

faqQuestions.q: Are Wegovy and Saxenda the same thing?
faqQuestions.a: No. Wegovy contains semaglutide (newer, more potent) while Saxenda contains liraglutide (older, first-generation). Both are GLP-1 receptor agonists but different molecules.

faqQuestions.q: Which has better insurance coverage?
faqQuestions.a: Coverage varies by plan. Both are approved for weight management, but Saxenda has been available longer and may be on more formularies. Always check your specific plan.

medA.name: Compounded Semaglutide
uniqueCons: Legal status may change if shortages end

verdict: Compounded semaglutide offers the same molecule at 60-85% lower cost, making GLP-1 treatment accessible to more patients. Brand versions offer FDA oversight and manufacturing consistency. The FDA allows compounding during active shortages. Choose based on budget and your comfort level with compounding pharmacy sourcing.

bestFor.medA: Patients who need affordable access or cannot get brand due to shortages
bestFor.medB: Patients who want FDA-approved product with guaranteed manufacturing standards

headToHeadTrials: No head-to-head trials exist. Compounded versions use the same semaglutide molecule but are formulated by 503A/503B pharmacies.

faqQuestions.q: Is compounded semaglutide safe?
faqQuestions.a: When sourced from licensed 503A or 503B pharmacies, compounded semaglutide uses the same active molecule. Look for pharmacies with USP 797/800 compliance and third-party testing.

faqQuestions.q: Is compounded semaglutide as effective as Ozempic?
faqQuestions.a: The active ingredient is the same. However, compounded versions may use different salts (e.g., semaglutide sodium vs acetate) and formulations, which could affect absorption. Most patients report comparable results.

faqQuestions.q: Will compounded semaglutide be banned?
faqQuestions.a: Compounding is allowed during FDA-declared shortages. If the shortage ends, compounders may need to stop production of copies. However, compounders can still make modified formulations like sublingual versions.

verdict: Compounded tirzepatide offers massive cost savings ($150-450 vs $1,000+/mo) with the same active molecule. Brand versions provide FDA-approved manufacturing and convenient pre-filled pens. Eli Lilly has been more aggressive in challenging compounders, so availability may be less stable than compounded semaglutide.

bestFor.medA: Cost-conscious patients comfortable with compounding pharmacy sourcing
bestFor.medB: Patients who want manufacturer-guaranteed quality and FDA-approved dosing devices

headToHeadTrials: No head-to-head trials. Same molecule, different manufacturing process.

faqQuestions.q: Is compounded tirzepatide the same as Mounjaro?
faqQuestions.a: It uses the same active molecule (tirzepatide) but is produced by compounding pharmacies rather than Eli Lilly. The formulation, concentration, and delivery device may differ.

faqQuestions.q: Why is compounded tirzepatide so much cheaper?
faqQuestions.a: Compounding pharmacies don't have the same R&D, marketing, and regulatory costs as brand manufacturers. They also operate under different FDA oversight during drug shortages.

faqQuestions.q: How do I verify a compounding pharmacy is legitimate?
faqQuestions.a: Check for state board of pharmacy licensure, 503A or 503B registration with the FDA, USP 797/800 compliance, and third-party testing certificates for potency and sterility.

medA.name: Oral Semaglutide
uniqueCons: Strict fasting requirements (30 min before food/water)

verdict: Injectable semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy) produces roughly twice the weight loss of oral semaglutide (Rybelsus) — ~15% vs ~5-8%. The oral form is ideal for patients who cannot tolerate injections, but the injectable form is far superior for weight loss. A higher-dose oral semaglutide (25mg and 50mg) is in development and may close this gap.

bestFor.medA: Needle-averse patients or those wanting a pill-based GLP-1 option
bestFor.medB: Patients prioritizing maximum weight loss with weekly dosing convenience

headToHeadTrials: PIONEER studies tested oral doses up to 14mg. Higher oral doses (25mg, 50mg) showed weight loss closer to injectable in Phase 3 trials.

faqQuestions.q: Is Rybelsus as effective as Ozempic for weight loss?
faqQuestions.a: No. Rybelsus at its current max dose (14mg) produces about 5-8% weight loss, while injectable Ozempic/Wegovy produces 10-17%. The oral form has lower bioavailability.

faqQuestions.q: Can I take Rybelsus instead of injecting Ozempic?
faqQuestions.a: Yes, but expect less weight loss. Rybelsus requires strict fasting (take on empty stomach with max 4oz water, wait 30 minutes before eating). Discuss the trade-offs with your doctor.

faqQuestions.q: Is a higher-dose oral semaglutide coming?
faqQuestions.a: Yes. Novo Nordisk has developed oral semaglutide at 25mg and 50mg doses, which showed weight loss much closer to injectable versions in clinical trials. FDA approval is expected.

medA.name: Semaglutide
uniqueCons: Less weight loss than retatrutide (based on early data)

medB.name: Retatrutide
medB.keyTrial: Phase 2 (up to 24.2% weight loss at 48 weeks)

verdict: Retatrutide's Phase 2 data shows ~24% weight loss at 48 weeks — potentially exceeding even tirzepatide. However, it is not yet approved or available. Semaglutide is the proven, available option today. If retatrutide's Phase 3 trials confirm these results, it could become the most effective obesity medication.

bestFor.medA: Patients who need treatment now with a proven, available medication
bestFor.medB: Future option — currently only available through clinical trials

headToHeadTrials: No head-to-head trials yet. Retatrutide is in Phase 3 development by Eli Lilly.

faqQuestions.q: When will retatrutide be available?
faqQuestions.a: Retatrutide is currently in Phase 3 trials. If successful, FDA approval could come in 2026-2027. Until then, it is only available through clinical trials.

faqQuestions.q: Is retatrutide better than semaglutide?
faqQuestions.a: Early Phase 2 data suggests retatrutide may produce more weight loss (~24% vs ~15%), but Phase 3 data is needed to confirm. It also targets three receptors vs one for semaglutide.

faqQuestions.q: How does the triple agonist mechanism work?
faqQuestions.a: Retatrutide activates GLP-1 (appetite suppression), GIP (metabolic improvement), and glucagon (increased energy expenditure) receptors simultaneously, potentially creating a more comprehensive metabolic effect.

medA.name: Tirzepatide
uniqueCons: May be surpassed by next-gen drugs

medB.name: Retatrutide
medB.keyTrial: Phase 2 (up to 24.2% weight loss at 48 weeks)
uniquePros: May boost energy expenditure via glucagon

verdict: Both are Eli Lilly products. Tirzepatide is available now and highly effective (~22% weight loss). Retatrutide adds a third receptor (glucagon) and early data suggests slightly more weight loss (~24%), but it needs Phase 3 confirmation. Tirzepatide is the best available option today; retatrutide could be the future.

bestFor.medA: Patients who need the most effective available medication right now
bestFor.medB: Future option — currently in clinical trials, not commercially available

headToHeadTrials: No head-to-head trials. Both from Eli Lilly; retatrutide is in Phase 3 while tirzepatide is commercially available.

faqQuestions.q: Will retatrutide replace tirzepatide?
faqQuestions.a: It is unlikely to fully replace tirzepatide. They may serve different patient populations. Eli Lilly will likely market both, as they do with Mounjaro and Zepbound today.

faqQuestions.q: How much better is retatrutide than tirzepatide?
faqQuestions.a: Phase 2 data shows retatrutide achieving ~24% weight loss at 48 weeks vs tirzepatide's ~22% at 72 weeks. The additional glucagon component may also improve liver fat and metabolic health.

faqQuestions.q: Should I wait for retatrutide instead of starting tirzepatide?
faqQuestions.a: No. Retatrutide is likely 1-2+ years from approval. If you need treatment now, tirzepatide is an excellent and proven option. You could always switch later if retatrutide proves superior.

medB.name: Survodutide
medB.keyTrial: Phase 2 (up to 18.7% weight loss at 46 weeks)
uniqueCons: Slightly less weight loss than tirzepatide

verdict: Tirzepatide (GLP-1/GIP) is available now and achieves ~22% weight loss. Survodutide (GLP-1/glucagon) shows ~19% in Phase 2 with a different mechanism that may be especially beneficial for liver disease (MASH). They target different secondary receptors, so the best choice may depend on the patient's full metabolic profile.

bestFor.medA: Patients wanting the most effective available weight loss medication today
bestFor.medB: Future option, especially for patients with fatty liver disease (MASH/NASH)

headToHeadTrials: No direct comparison. Different mechanisms: tirzepatide targets GIP while survodutide targets glucagon.

faqQuestions.q: What is survodutide?
faqQuestions.a: Survodutide is a dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist being developed by Boehringer Ingelheim. Unlike tirzepatide (GLP-1/GIP), it pairs GLP-1 with glucagon, which may boost energy expenditure and improve liver fat.

faqQuestions.q: When will survodutide be available?
faqQuestions.a: Survodutide is in Phase 3 trials for obesity and MASH (fatty liver disease). Approval could come in 2026-2027 if trials succeed.

faqQuestions.q: How does survodutide differ from tirzepatide?
faqQuestions.a: Both use GLP-1, but tirzepatide adds GIP (improves insulin sensitivity) while survodutide adds glucagon (increases energy expenditure and reduces liver fat). They represent different approaches to dual-receptor therapy.

medB.name: Phentermine
uniqueCons: Not for patients with heart conditions

verdict: GLP-1 medications produce 2-4x more weight loss than phentermine with a completely different mechanism and safety profile. Phentermine is far cheaper ($10-30/mo vs $150-1,400/mo) but is limited to short-term use (12 weeks) and carries cardiovascular risks. GLP-1s represent a paradigm shift in obesity treatment.

bestFor.medA: Patients seeking significant, sustained weight loss with metabolic benefits
bestFor.medB: Patients needing short-term appetite suppression on a tight budget who have no cardiovascular risk

headToHeadTrials: STEP 9 compared semaglutide to phentermine-topiramate and showed comparable weight loss, though different side effect profiles.

faqQuestions.q: Are GLP-1s better than phentermine?
faqQuestions.a: For sustained weight loss, yes. GLP-1s produce significantly more weight loss (15-22% vs 5-7%), can be used long-term, and have metabolic benefits. Phentermine is limited to 12 weeks and carries stimulant risks.

faqQuestions.q: Can I take phentermine and a GLP-1 together?
faqQuestions.a: Some doctors prescribe them together, especially during the GLP-1 titration period. However, this is off-label and should only be done under medical supervision.

faqQuestions.q: Why would anyone choose phentermine over GLP-1s?
faqQuestions.a: Cost is the main reason — phentermine is $10-30/mo vs $150-1,400/mo for GLP-1s. It also works immediately and is a simple pill. Some patients use it as a bridge while waiting for GLP-1 access.

medB.name: Bariatric Surgery
medB.brands: Gastric Bypass / Gastric Sleeve / Duodenal Switch

verdict: Bariatric surgery produces more weight loss (25-35%) than GLP-1 medications (15-22%) and is a one-time procedure. However, GLP-1s are non-surgical, reversible, and have fewer acute risks. For patients with BMI >40 or severe comorbidities, surgery may still be the best option. GLP-1s have made non-surgical treatment viable for the first time at near-surgical levels of weight loss.

bestFor.medA: Patients who prefer non-surgical treatment, have BMI 27-40, or want a reversible approach
bestFor.medB: Patients with BMI >40 or severe obesity-related comorbidities who want maximum one-time weight loss

headToHeadTrials: STAMPEDE (10-year data) showed surgery superior for diabetes resolution. Newer studies comparing GLP-1s to surgery are ongoing.

faqQuestions.q: Can GLP-1s replace bariatric surgery?
faqQuestions.a: For many patients with BMI 30-40, GLP-1 medications may achieve sufficient weight loss without surgery. For severe obesity (BMI >40) or when comorbidities require maximum weight loss, surgery may still be preferable.

faqQuestions.q: Which is more cost-effective long-term?
faqQuestions.a: Surgery has a high upfront cost ($15,000-35,000) but is one-time. GLP-1s cost $1,800-16,800/year indefinitely. Over 5-10 years, the cumulative cost of GLP-1s can exceed surgery costs.

faqQuestions.q: Can I use GLP-1s after bariatric surgery?
faqQuestions.a: Yes, some patients use GLP-1 medications after surgery if they experience weight regain. This combination approach is becoming more common and is supported by emerging clinical data.

medB.name: Orforglipron
medB.brandCost: Not yet available (expected to be cheaper than injectables)
medB.keyTrial: Phase 2 (up to 14.7% weight loss at 36 weeks)
uniquePros: Small molecule (easier to manufacture, no cold chain)
May be cheaper to produce at scale
uniqueCons: Slightly less weight loss in early data

verdict: Orforglipron could be a game-changer as an oral small-molecule GLP-1 that approaches injectable semaglutide's weight loss (~14% vs ~15%). Unlike Rybelsus (oral semaglutide), orforglipron is a small molecule that is easier and cheaper to manufacture. If approved, it could dramatically lower the cost of GLP-1 therapy and eliminate the need for injections.

bestFor.medA: Patients who need effective treatment available today
bestFor.medB: Future option for patients who want injectable-level results from a daily pill

headToHeadTrials: No head-to-head trials yet. Phase 3 trials (ACHIEVE program) are underway.

faqQuestions.q: When will orforglipron be available?
faqQuestions.a: Eli Lilly is conducting Phase 3 trials (ACHIEVE program). If successful, FDA approval could come in 2026-2027. It would be the first oral small-molecule GLP-1 for obesity.

faqQuestions.q: How is orforglipron different from Rybelsus?
faqQuestions.a: Rybelsus is oral semaglutide (a peptide that needs special absorption enhancement and fasting). Orforglipron is a small molecule — it absorbs more easily, doesn't require fasting, and is much cheaper to manufacture.

faqQuestions.q: Could orforglipron make GLP-1s affordable?
faqQuestions.a: Potentially, yes. Small molecules are far cheaper to produce than peptides. Without cold chain requirements and with simpler manufacturing, the cost could be significantly lower than current injectable GLP-1s.

medA.name: Mounjaro
uniquePros: Better insurance coverage for diabetic patients

medB.name: Zepbound
uniqueCons: Same molecule as Mounjaro at higher cost

verdict: Mounjaro and Zepbound are the same drug (tirzepatide) by Eli Lilly, just like Ozempic and Wegovy are both semaglutide. Mounjaro is approved for type 2 diabetes; Zepbound is approved for weight management. The medication, device, and doses are identical. The difference is labeling, insurance coverage, and the prescribing context.

bestFor.medA: Patients with type 2 diabetes who will benefit from insurance coverage under the diabetes indication
bestFor.medB: Patients without diabetes who need the FDA-approved obesity indication for insurance or clinical purposes

headToHeadTrials: No head-to-head needed — identical drug. SURPASS trials (diabetes) and SURMOUNT trials (obesity) both used tirzepatide.

faqQuestions.q: Are Mounjaro and Zepbound the same drug?
faqQuestions.a: Yes, both contain tirzepatide made by Eli Lilly. Mounjaro is approved for type 2 diabetes, while Zepbound is approved for weight management. The auto-injector, doses, and medication are identical.

faqQuestions.q: Why would my doctor prescribe one over the other?
faqQuestions.a: If you have type 2 diabetes, you would be prescribed Mounjaro (diabetes indication). If you are seeking weight management without diabetes, you would be prescribed Zepbound (obesity indication). This affects insurance coverage.

faqQuestions.q: Can I switch between Mounjaro and Zepbound?
faqQuestions.a: Since they are the same medication, switching is seamless — same dose, same schedule. Your doctor might switch you if your insurance situation changes or if you develop/resolve type 2 diabetes.
